chunks.text: We review and report individual schools and services and also look at how our education system's delivering nationally. 2024 to 25 was a successful year for ero, where we reviewed nearly a third of all schools and early learning services in New Zealand. We delivered five national reports, including NCA, relationships and sexuality education, chronic truancy counseling schools in oral language.

Recently, since the end of that year, we've also published reports on attendance, English and maths and cell phones. And I'm happy to answer questions on those. In doing this work, we visited and reviewed 745 schools in Kura, over 1,200 EC services. And we had over 60,000 respondents from teachers, parents, boards, leaders to our surveys.

And over 2000 interviews with teachers, leaders, parents and students. Three quarters of the schools and services inform us that our reports help shape their decisions and their practice, which is really encouraging. And positively. So this year Eero actually saw encouraging signs of improvement in education. This I'd like to say, is thanks to the hard work we saw of teachers in classrooms up and down the country, and they should be recognized. This year we saw more early learning services meet the expected quality standards that Euro sets.

So that was 53% compared to 36% in the previous year. We saw attendance improved to pre COVID levels, and for the first time we now saw students thinking going to school every day is more important. This is encouraging. Eero assessed a quarter of schools as having improved in teaching and learning, and a third having improved in curriculum and leadership.

And our national reviews found early signs of improvement in English and maths and primary schools with half of teachers reporting an improvement. But chair there is further to go ERA remains very concerned in three areas. The first is the variable quality of early childhood education. 47% of services we reviewed in that year did not meet our quality threshold and we need to do better for our youngest children.

Our secondary of concern is schools that are struggling to deliver the quality of education. In 24 25, we had ongoing work in 168 schools of concern across the country and we recommended intervention by the ministry in 32 schools. These schools that are struggling are a concern to us because of the impact that they have on students'

opportunities, students' learning, and students' future. And an era that's the heart of what we do, is making sure that every child, no matter where they live, gets access to equality education. The third area of concern is while we are seeing encouraging signs, there is still a large mountain to climb in terms of student achievement. In 2024, only a quarter of year eight students were at the level expected for maths and writing and only half for reading.

'cause I, whilst this is backward looking, I wanted to share with you how excited we are about the year ahead 'cause of the greater impact we could potentially have.
